artwork_file: Charles Marion Russell_1921_When Mules Wear Diamonds.jpg
artwork_description: Painted in *1921*, Charles Marion Russell (1864–1926) crafted *When Mules Wear Diamonds* in *Oil on canvas* with an emphasis on visual clarity and structure. Subtle modulations and strategic simplifications organize the image, allowing key motifs to register with immediacy. The work’s lasting appeal lies in its ability to translate observation into a composed, enduring form.

artwork_file: Charles Marion Russell_1905_Lewis and Clark on the Lower Columbia.jpg
artwork_title: Lewis and Clark on the Lower Columbia
artwork_description: Created in *1905*, Charles Marion Russell (1864–1926) produced *Lewis and Clark on the Lower Columbia* in *Oil on canvas*. The work privileges composition and surface effects, guiding the viewer through deliberate rhythms of form and tone. Rather than offering a purely descriptive account, it emphasizes choices of structure, emphasis, and visual pacing. Seen today, *Lewis and Clark on the Lower Columbia* distills the artist’s sensibility into a concentrated image.

In *1911*, Charles Marion Russell (1864–1926) realized *The Medicine Man* in *Oil on canvas*. Attention to balance and interval—between figures and ground, or between mass and void—creates the work’s quiet authority. The composition’s internal logic invites viewers to read the image as an arrangement of relations, not simply a scene. *The Medicine Man* stands as a focused statement of the artist’s formal intelligence.

*The Custer Fight* (*1903*) exemplifies Charles Marion Russell (1864–1926) at a moment when pictorial decisions—scale, placement, and contour—shape meaning as much as subject matter. Working in *Oil on canvas*, the artist orchestrates contrasts and transitions to hold the eye in slow movement across the surface. The result is an image that rewards close looking, where small shifts of texture and edge carry expressive weight.
